persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: the bio is flagged as a single-initial namesake trap, and 'Clerk' is a generic junior title, so the 1889-1907 stint could in principle be a different E. Bonavia (12 Bonavias in the staff lists); the bio also never prints the middle initial 'L'. Why this fails: the 1896 staff list prints the full forename 'Edgar L. Bonavia', and the career matches the printed biography clause-for-clause across two decades and two stints: post office clerk (bio 1886; staff list 1889 'Clerk, Post Office'), transfer to the secretariat 1894 (1896-1905 clerk in the Chief Secretary's Office), Lieutenant Governor's Office by 1906-07, then 'dep. asst. sec. to govt., 1911' (1912 'Deputy Assistant Secretary'), 'asst. sec. to govt., 1912' (1913-1921 'Assistant Secretary'), the shared C.M.G. honour (bio 1917; staff list from 1918), and 'perm. sec. to head of ministry, 5th Nov., 1921' (1922-1925 'Permanent Secretary, Office of the Head of the Ministry'). These are many independent agreeing dimensions (full forename, specific successive positions, exact departments, dates, honour); no rival Bonavia hypothesis survives. The two stints are continuous segments of the same documented career, with the 1908-1911 gap in stint coverage bridged by the bio's 'stenographer to the coun. of govt., 1891-1911'.

evidence_cited.fact: Office of the Head of the Ministry

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second 'Edgar Bonavia' in Maltese government service in the same decades (e.g., a son with the same forename serving in the secretariat), or a printed source showing the C.M.G. of 1917 was awarded to a different E. Bonavia.

unassigned_stints.reason: Printed as 'Miss E. Bonavia' -- a woman, so she cannot be Edgar Bonavia. Likely a relative (the shared Lieutenant-Governor's Office department is suggestive of family connection, not identity). No tenure overlap with the bio, and Edgar would be 66-69 in 1934-37 appearing as a junior Shorthand Typist, which is implausible.

notes: The two Edgar L. Bonavia stints (1889-1907 and 1912-1925) are clearly the same man as the biography; the deterministic position_sim failures are string-similarity artifacts ('clk., post office' vs 'Second Class Clerk'; 'boy scouts' comsnr.' vs 'Assistant Secretary') -- the verbatim texts agree precisely once abbreviations are expanded. The 'Miss E. Bonavia' stint should be kept as a separate person record.
